Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a binary package sourced from a Debian `.deb` file hosted at `https://prerelease.keybase.io`, a domain associated with the Keybase project. The `source` array fetches the `.deb` file directly, and checksums are provided via `sha256sums_x86_64`, allowing verification against known-good values. The `pkgver` is hardcoded and does not involve dynamic fetching or command substitution that could introduce remote code execution during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`.

During the sourcing of the PKGBUILD (which includes evaluating `pkgver()` and top-level expressions), no dangerous operations occur: there are no command substitutions, remote executions, or network requests embedded in variable assignments. The modifications to `pkgver` are simple string replacements and do not invoke external commands. The use of `http(s)` to download a `.deb` from the official Keybase distribution point is standard for `-bin` packages and consistent with AUR packaging norms.

The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are skipped due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so any actions inside them (e.g., extracting `.deb` contents) are not executed at this stage. Thus, sourcing the PKGBUILD and downloading the source as part of `makepkg --printsrcinfo` poses no risk of executing malicious payloads or exfiltrating data.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code execution during source fetch[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a prebuilt Debian package from Keybase's official prerelease server (prerelease.keybase.io) and extracts it into the package directory, which is a standard practice for `-bin` packages in AUR. The source URL is consistent with Keybase's documented infrastructure, and the checksum provided can be cross-verified with their official Debian repository metadata, as noted in the comment. No dynamic or untrusted downloads occur during the build process.

The package removes cron.daily entries from the Debian package, which is a reasonable adaptation for an Arch Linux environment where systemd timers or manual user configuration are preferred. This modification does not introduce malicious behavior. All dependencies and provided/conflicting packages are appropriate for the Keybase client suite. There is no obfuscated code, suspicious network activity, or unauthorized system modifications.

The file adheres to standard AUR packaging practices for binary packages. The source is from the official vendor, the checksum is present and verifiable, and the build process is transparent and minimal. While the pkgver uses a pre-release versioning scheme, this reflects the upstream's distribution model and not a supply-chain risk.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package from official source
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing keybase.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package from official source
LLM auditresponse for keybase.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `keybase.install` script contains only `post_install` and `post_upgrade` functions that execute a script located at `/opt/keybase/post_install.sh`. This pattern is standard practice in Arch Linux packages, especially for binaries distributed via AUR or vendor-provided packages. The executed script is assumed to be part of the Keybase application bundle installed under `/opt`, which is a common location for third-party or self-contained software.

Calling an internal post-install script during package installation or upgrade is a normal method for performing application-specific setup tasks, such as desktop file registration, service enabling, or permissions adjustments—all of which are within expected packaging behavior. There is no evidence of obfuscated code, unauthorized network activity, or execution of external payloads. The actions taken are limited in scope and consistent with post-installation configuration.

While the actual security of the execution depends on the integrity of the `/opt/keybase/post_install.sh` script (which is outside the scope of this install file), the install script itself does not introduce malicious behavior. No suspicious downloads, eval statements, or file exfiltration occur within this file.
</details>
<summary>
Standard post-install script invocation; no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

[0/1] Reviewing src/opt/keybase/post_install.sh...
LLM auditresponse for post_install.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `post_install.sh` script performs standard post-installation tasks for the Keybase package on Linux systems. It manages the `/keybase` mountpoint, adjusts file permissions, restarts systemd services if needed, and fixes known issues from previous versions (such as incorrect file ownership in user config directories). All operations are confined to expected paths (`/keybase`, `/home/*/.config/keybase`, `/usr/bin/keybase-redirector`, etc.) and serve the legitimate functionality of the Keybase application.

The script interacts with systemd user units, updates desktop integration (via `update-mime-database` and `gtk-update-icon-cache`), and conditionally restarts Keybase services when safe to do so. It includes protections against restarting services while filesystem mounts are in use. There is no network activity, no code execution from remote sources, no obfuscation, and no attempts to exfiltrate data. Actions like `chown`, `chmod`, `killall`, and `su` are used appropriately in context and align with system service management needs.

Some operations involve privilege escalation (e.g., changing binary permissions to suid via `chmod 4755`), but this is consistent with the redirector's requirement for root privileges to manage filesystem mounts. The script checks for secure conditions before applying changes and logs actions for transparency. No malicious or suspicious behavior was found.
</details>
<summary>
Standard post-install Keybase service management; no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed src/opt/keybase/post_install.sh. Status: SAFE -- Standard post-install Keybase service management; no malicious code

LLM auditresponse for 102.bundle.js:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard minified webpack chunk for the Keybase application. It exports React components for channel selection and team repo settings, using Keybase's internal state management (e.g., `useTeamsState`, `useConvoState`) and RPC functions (`localGetTLFConversationsLocalRpcPromise`) to fetch team conversations. The code is typical frontend JavaScript for a chat/team management UI. There are no obfuscated strings, no suspicious network requests (beyond the application's own RPCs), no eval or base64 decoding, and no file system or system modifications. The minified format is expected for webpack bundles. No evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Keybase webpack chunk, no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for post_install.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard post-install script for the Keybase binary package. It manages the `/keybase` redirector mountpoint, sets SUID permissions on the `keybase-redirector` binary, restarts systemd services for running Keybase instances (if autorestart is enabled), and handles upgrades from older installations (e.g., replacing symlinks, cleaning up legacy mounts). It also fixes bad permissions that could have been created by a previous bug in a similar script. All operations are confined to Keybase’s own binaries, configuration directories, and standard system caches (MIME, icon). There are no network requests, no code fetched or executed from unexpected sources, no obfuscation, and no exfiltration of data. The use of `su`, `killall`, `systemctl`, and `chown`/`chmod` is normal for a post-install script managing system-wide and per-user Keybase services. Nothing in this file deviates from legitimate packaging or maintenance behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Keybase post-install script; no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard frontend JavaScript bundle for the Keybase application. It contains React components that render a proof-posting workflow (for identity proofs on platforms like Twitter, GitHub, Reddit, HackerNews, etc.). The code uses webpack module loading and React runtime, importing common adapters and utilities. It constructs URLs to social media sites and opens them in the browser when the user interacts with the UI — this is expected application functionality, not malicious. There are no obfuscated strings, no `eval`, no `base64` decoding, no unexpected network fetches (the only external URLs are the user's own proof post targets). The file does not contain any code that exfiltrates data, downloads or executes binaries, performs unauthorized system modifications, or deviates from the Keybase application’s stated purpose. It is a typical UI module for managing cryptographic identity proofs.
</details>
